coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid capped white luminescent carbon dots: application in sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Mehra, S.
Khandare, S.D.
Singh, K.
Chaudhary, D.R.
Kumar, A.
coredata.dc:description: A new category of white luminescent materials, white-light-emitting carbon dots (CDs), has gained massive attention due to their exclusive features and potential applications. The complicated structure of CDs confuses the understanding of their photoluminescence (PL) mechanism, which is still unclear and a significant challenge to interpret the surface chemistry. This work compares the hydrothermal and ionic liquid (IL) solvothermal approaches to producing high-quality CDs. The modulation of solvent with IL and doping amount of nitrogen gives the white-light-emitting CDs. In particular, the CDs-IL exhibits novel and robust white PL performance. IL surface modification led to PL enhancement with a red shift from 543 nm to 620 nm and an increase in the quantum yield from 16.8 ± 2% to 68.6 ± 2%. The enhancement in PL of CDs-IL inspired us to utilize them as bioimaging probes on bacterial cell lines and sensing probes for water pollutants Fe3+ and trinitrophenol, lower the detection limit 0.117 ± 0.010 μM and 0.049 ± 0.005 μM. These results confirmed that IL capped on the surface functional group of CDs forms a stable structure surrounding the CDs. Our finding provides clear evidence for the surface chemistry-dependent PL behavior of CDs.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid capped white luminescent carbon dots: application in sensing and bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new category of white luminescent materials, white-light-emitting carbon dots (CDs), has gained massive attention due to their exclusive features and potential applications. The complicated structure of CDs confuses the understanding of their photoluminescence (PL) mechanism, which is still unclear and a significant challenge to interpret the surface chemistry. This work compares the hydrothermal and ionic liquid (IL) solvothermal approaches to producing high-quality CDs. The modulation of solvent with IL and doping amount of nitrogen gives the white-light-emitting CDs. In particular, the CDs-IL exhibits novel and robust white PL performance. IL surface modification led to PL enhancement with a red shift from 543 nm to 620 nm and an increase in the quantum yield from 16.8 ± 2% to 68.6 ± 2%. The enhancement in PL of CDs-IL inspired us to utilize them as bioimaging probes on bacterial cell lines and sensing probes for water pollutants Fe3+ and trinitrophenol, lower the detection limit 0.117 ± 0.010 μM and 0.049 ± 0.005 μM. These results confirmed that IL capped on the surface functional group of CDs forms a stable structure surrounding the CDs. Our finding provides clear evidence for the surface chemistry-dependent PL behavior of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have earned great attention due to their tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, ease of fabrication, cheap natural raw materials, good biocompatibility, photostability, and plenty of surface chemical groups [1–3]. Based on these excellent properties, CDs have shown a variety of applications in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, light-emitting diodes, information encryption, sensors, and photocatalysis [4–7]. CDs are ‘zero-dimensional’ with a size of less than 10 nm and a popular class in the extensive quantum dots family. The first discovery of CDs was observed during the purification of carbon nanotubes from a crude mixture in 2004 by Xu et al., and in 2006, Sun et al. termed the name ‘carbon dots’ [8,9]. CDs have been intensively studied, including the various synthetic methods, characterizations approaches, and applications in multiple fields [10–15]. CDs are fascinating to researchers for their applications in chemistry and biomedicine [16]. The PL mechanism of CDs is still not precise. It is mainly considered that surface state (traps and defects) PL and intrinsic state PL are the two main transitions approaches responsible for the PL of CDs. The surface state PL is due to carbon bonds and surface chemical groups, which usually show excitation wavelength-dependent PL properties. The intrinsic PL state is highly related to the carbon-core, which is generally independent of the excitation wavelength [17]. To explain the complex PL properties of CDs, various mechanisms have been projected, like defects in surface state, quantum effect, size of the intrinsic core, edge zigzag state, and electron–hole recombination [18–25]. In multiple reports, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs approached 10%, while more complicated synthetic protocols reached 20–30% QY; only a few recent demonstrations have crossed 50% [26]. Some research groups reported a QY of over 90%, which is quite challenging [27,28]. Finding new approaches to enhance the QYs of CDs can further extend the choices of their practical applicability and integrate these materials into a widely used technology.

However, CDs have superior properties, which make them superior to metal-based quantum dots. The fabrication of light-emitting diodes using CDs, with a color rendering index larger than 80 is in high demand [29]. The PL characteristics of CDs depend on their sizes, functional groups, and surface traps [30,31]. Different sizes of CDs have a different distribution of sp2 structure inside the core and disturb the electronic energy level with a consequent variation in their PL properties [9,32]. Various reports on synthetic methods show that the carbon source and solvent selections are critical parameters for controlling the size of CDs [33–35].

Ionic liquids (ILs) are molten salts consisting of various anions, and organic cations have attracted extensive attention from researchers due to their unique properties [19–21]. ILs were widely used to synthesize many nanomaterials due to their high solubilizability, tunable acidity, designable molecularity, and high thermal stability [36]. ILs play a key role in regulating the size of CDs, structure, and surface traps with enhanced PL performance due to heteroatom atomic structure [37–40]. The low interfacial tension and low surface energy of ILs play a crucial role in accelerating the nucleation frequency and forming nanoparticles with narrow distribution [41]. Herein, some research on the fabrication of CDs using ILs and PL quantum yield (PLQY) of the resulting CDs was low, and the PL was mainly in the blue region [42]. For instance, the size of CDs changed by varying ILs from 0.6 to 1.6 nm [43]. Castro et al. modified the surface of CDs using halogenated imidazolium ILs with different sizes of 1.5–3.0 nm. The variation in the size is mainly associated with the cation part of the ILs, while the anion effect is negligible [44]. It was assumed that the imidazolium cations around CDs could generate electrostatic repulsion and extensity and reduce the agglomeration of CDs. Conventionally, CDs with oxygenated functional groups on the surface show lower PL than nitrogen-doped CDs. The electronic structure of nitrogen is similar to carbon, and its unpaired electrons can act as electron donors to improve the optical properties of CDs [4,45–47]. Furthermore, the surface passivation by amine groups and nitrogen doping into CDs core improved the PL properties more effectively [46,48]. However, surface modification and heteroatom doping apparently improved the sensitivity of the CDs to afford a new class of luminescent materials. Such materials have a vast number of active sites or functional groups for better sensing platform performance [49]. The surface-modifying strategies are crucial for successfully creating a luminescent material required for end-use applications. Because of the rising demand for energy-efficient displays, the synthesis of highly efficient and stable white-light-emitting materials with characteristics equivalent to bright sunshine is receiving exceptional attention [50–53]. Generally, white-light-emitting materials are made up either by (i) mixing red, green, and blue emitting materials or (ii) using multistep surface modification tactics. However, the uses of these tactics are limited due to their problems like non-radiative energy transfer, alteration in chromaticity, self-absorption, more expensive, and environmental toxicity [54]. Thus, the inexpensive and eco-friendly surface modification tactics gained more attention for synthesizing highly efficient white-light-emitting materials. CDs have become popular in various fields due to negligible photobleaching, photoblinking, and biocompatibility [55]. Bioimaging is achieved in two broad categories; in-vitro imaging of different cells and in-vivo imaging of vital cell organelles applications that depend on the penetration of CDs in mice and zebrafish [16].

CDs as sensing probe systems are preferable due to analyte-induced PL quenching. Although some CDs show selectivity toward interesting analytes such as Fe3+, Cu2+, ascorbic acid, polysaccharides, tartrazine, and quercetin, many need to be functionalized with desired molecules such as antibodies and aptamers to deliver high selectivity toward analytes of interest [56,57]. The surface functionalities of CDs related to the hydrophilicity and PL must be optimized concerning biocompatibility, sensitivity, and selectivity.

The ultrasensitive detection of nitroaromatic compounds (NAs) has become a more critical worldwide concern; the existence of these compounds depletes the environmental life cycle [58–61]. There are so many reports that have been published on fluorometric sensing of NA compounds [62–64]. CDs show a strong PL quenching effect with NAs, leading to the development of CDs sensor for NA detection [65]. CDs have been utilized as a probe to sense metal ions and explosive NAs in aqueous media. Trinitrophenol (TNP) is the most potent explosive that has been widely used in landmines for decades [66]. However, the vast use and high water solubility have denoted TNP as a serious environmental pollutant [67,68]. Therefore, emerging simple and effective ways of detecting TNP sensitivity are of significant consequence [69]. Among various detecting protocols, fluorescence-based explosives detection has gained popularity due to high sensitivity, portability, ultra-fast, and easy modularity.

Analyte-induced PL quenching of CDs is common via the inner filter effect and electron transfer. The interactions like coordination of their surface functional groups, including carboxylate and amino, with metal ions. Once the CDs interact with the metal ions, their PL can be quenched through electron transfer or the inner filter effect [70,71]. Further, Wang et al. reported the detection of Fe3+ ions by selective fluorescence quenching of CDs [72]. Fe3+ is an essential metal ion present in environmental and biological systems bonded with different sites of proteins present in biological systems. Fe3+ ions content plays a vital role in manipulating Parkinson's disease since its accumulation has affected the neurons of patients having Parkinson's disease [73]. Moreover, Fe3+ ions are among the significant pollutants in the environment and induce water pollution [74]. In response to all the above reasons, bioimaging and the sensitive detection of TNP and Fe3+ ions are urgently mandated.

Our work presents a straightforward way to synthesize ultrahigh PLQY of CDs by in-situ surface modification using a protic ethylammonium nitrate (EAN) IL as a surface modifier. Keeping this in mind, we designed a general and straightforward one-pot solvothermal strategy to synthesize white-light-emitting CDs. N-doping content and degree of defect formation on the surface of CDs can be regulated by synthetic conditions, such as a reaction solvent system. Excellent white PL characteristics of CDs can be achieved simultaneously in IL media. Based on the optical performance, we investigate the applications of CDs for bioimaging for bacterial cells and fluorometric sensing of TNP and Fe3+ pollutants. Advanced information encryption is successfully demonstrated.

Alginic acid from brown algae and 1,4-benzene-diamine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich India. Ethylamine (70% solution in water) and nitric acid (65% solution in water) and metal salts (LiCl, KCl, NiCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, BaCl·H2O, NaCl, CrCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3), AlCl3·6H2O) were purchased from SD fine chemicals Ltd. TNP from CDH Ltd and dialysis tubing from Sigma was procured. Fluorescein and cresyl violet acetate (70%) dyes were purchased from SRL Pvt Ltd. Deionized water was collected from the Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q, Millipore) and used throughout the study. Zobell marine broth (ZMB), Zobell marine agar, and medium and 16 mm tissue culture plates were procured from HiMedia, Pune, India. Artemia salina cysts were purchased from the local market of Bhavnagar.

Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrums were recorded using a Varian Cary 500 UV–vis near infrared spectrophotometer. PL spectrums were recorded using an Edinburgh F900 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed using a nanosecond diode laser of 400 nm as an excitation source on the Edinburgh instrument OB-920 spectrofluorometer. A single exponential iterative fitting program analyzed the fluorescence decay curves for all lifetime measurements. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a PAN Analytical EMPYREAN, Netherland instrument of source 40 kV and 30 mA, with Ni-filter Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiations. Infrared spectrums were recorded on the PerkinElmer GX Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) instrument. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were recorded on a JEOL, JEM 2100 electron microscope at 20 KeV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrums were recorded on a Thermo-scientific ESCALAB-250 Xi photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray as an excitation source outfitted on an X-ray spot size of 650 × 650 μm2, and resulting profiles were fitted using Avantage software. A macro zoom fluorescence microscope AX10 Axioimager M1, Carl Zeiss AG from Germany, was used to bioimage bacterial strains.

EAN was synthesized as per our earlier work [75]. In brief, an aqueous solution of monoethylamine (>70% in water) was taken in a round bottom flask, and the temperature was maintained at <5 °C using an ice bath. An equimolar amount of nitric acid (>65% in water) was added dropwise in the round bottom flask by an addition funnel. After complete addition, the reaction mixture was stirred in an ice bath until the reaction mixture temperature reached room temperature and kept for 24 h for completion of the reaction. The resultant EAN water mixture was dried under a vacuum using a rotary evaporator and stored in a vacuum desiccator containing CaCl2 as the desiccant. The purity of the EAN was confirmed by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (ESI-Fig. S1).

The CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal molecular fusion method using alginic acid (0.150 g) from brown algae as a precursor, 1,4-phenylenediamine (0.150 g) as a linker between alginic units homogenized by slow heating in two different solvent systems as EAN IL and water, in 20 mL were separately transferred to two poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves (50 mL) separately and heated at 180 °C for 6 h (Scheme 1 ) [76]. Collected crude mixtures were sonicated for 30 min and centrifuged at 7000 rpm to remove the carbonized precipitate. The supernatant solutions were obtained and dialyzed with deionized water in a dialysis bag (2000 D); after dialyzation, the supernatant was freeze-dried and obtained CDs powder stored at 2 °C for further characterization.

For QY measurements, fluorescein dye (QY = 79%) dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH solution (refractive index η = 1.77, 25 °C) and cresyl violet acetate dye (QY = 54%) dissolved in methanol (refractive index η = 1.32, 25 °C) were used as a reference for CDs-W and CDs-IL, respectively [77]. The PLQYs of CDs-W and CDs-IL were calculated by the relative method to the fluorescence intensity ratio (Fluorescein, λex = 498 nm) with absorbance and viscosity ratio. All samples were prepared in water, and their UV–vis absorbance at 400 and 420 nm was maintained to be less than 0.1. The relative PLQY was calculated from the equation provided in ESI-Eq. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response to TNP and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of TNP and Fe3+ were diluted into a certain amount of deionized water and added to 2.0 mL of CDs-IL dispersion. The CDs dispersion was titrated with 1.0 mM TNP and 2.0 mM Fe3+ stock solution at room temperature, and PL was recorded at excitation wavelengths of 420 nm. The fluorescence quenching rate was evaluated using the Stern–Volmer equation [78,79].

The thermodynamic parameters of the interactions of TNP and Fe3+ with CDs-IL are evaluated by isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). Heat is generated or absorbed when a molecule binds with others; it was measured by a MicroCal ITC200 microcalorimeter. The titration was done with a 40 μL Hamiltonian syringe associated with the instrument by adding 1.0 μL aliquots of TNP (1.0 mM) and Fe3+ (2.0 mM) stock solution into the sample cell containing 200 μL of CDs dispersion with continuous stirring (500 rpm). The addition was controlled by ITC200 software provided with the instrument. Each addition's enthalpy change at 25 °C was measured and plotted against concentration using origin software linked with ITC200. The binding isotherm of TNP and Fe3+ with CDs-IL was used to define the concentration regimes of CDs-IL with both analytes interactions. The experimental data were fitted into a one-site binding model, and changes in enthalpy (ΔH), entropy (ΔS), and free energy of binding (ΔG) were calculated by ITC200 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory activity of CDs on bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibitory activity of CDs on bacterial cells was checked using three bacterial cultures (Vibrio sp., Bacillus subtilis, Pseudomonas sp.). The single colony from all bacterial cultures was inoculated in ZMB and grown overnight at 30 °C. This overnight grown culture (1%) was plated on Zobell marine agar plate and added 100 ppm dispersion of CDs by making holes in the middle of Petri plates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging study of CDs on bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the bioimaging potential of both CDs, bacterial cells (Vibrio sp. and Pseudomonas sp.) were grown in ZMB medium till mid-log phase (0.5–0.6 of OD600) at 30 °C. The developed bacterial cells were centrifuged at room temperature for 5 min at 5000 rpm. To improve the clarity of the bacterial cells for imaging, the resulting pellets were washed twice with 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (7.0 pH) to eliminate any cell debris and waste material. Re-suspending, the pellet in the same sodium phosphate buffer, creating the smear on a glass slide free of grease, and letting it air dry were all done. For 10 min, PL CDs solution was used to stain the dried smear. The stained-glass slide mounted on an Epifluorescence microscope (AX10 Axioimager M1, Carl Zeiss, Germany) is equipped with a different filter to take PL images of bacterial cells using inbuilt Axio Vision software. A slide without any CDs stain containing only bacterial smear was prepared for control images under normal light conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acute toxicity analysis of CDs on the Artemia salina
ce:section.ce:para:
The toxicity of CDs was analyzed, and healthy Artemia salina cysts were obtained from the local hatchery market. These cysts were suspended in filtered and sterilized normal seawater for hatching with a 60 Watt lamp with an ambient temperature of 24–26 °C for 50 h of incubation. For toxicity analysis, 20 live larvae were transferred into 10 mL seawater after hatching in each well of the plate (6 well flat bottoms, 16 mm tissue culture plates, HiMedia, Pune) with increasing concentrations of CDs with negative control (without CDs). All plates were incubated under normal illumination for 50 h [80]. All treatment in the toxicity assay was carried out in triplicates. To check the toxicity of CDs mortality rate of each treatment was calculated below the given equation:
M o r t a l i t y
( % )
=
M i − M f
M i

100
Where Mi = Mortality before treatment, Mf = Mortality after treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: IL, EAN, and water were selected to prepare CDs under the same synthesis environment to systematically investigate the size, morphology control, and surface passivation phenomenon by IL. TEM was used to characterize the morphology and particle size of prepared CDs. CDs-IL exhibit a uniform spherical distribution with an average particle size of 2.2 nm and 0.21 nm lattice spacing with an interfringes distance [81]. CDs-W has an average size distribution of 5.2 nm with 0.23 nm interplanar distance, and less uniformity was attained compared to CDs-IL (see Fig. 1). The difference in particle size and uniformity originated from the difference in the solvent media. The dominant amorphous character of the CDs is also reflected in the broad hump centered around ∼2θ = 22° in the XRD profile shown in Fig. 2 A. Such a hump has been widely observed in XRD patterns of amorphous carbon [82]. The XRD patterns of synthesized CDs-IL and CDs-W (Fig. 2a) show a board diffraction peak at around 21.6° and 22.1°, respectively, signifying a (002) and near about 42° for (100) layer spacing of the graphite planes with a highly disordered carbon atoms arrangement. FTIR, FT-Raman, and XPS experiments were carried out to investigate the functionality on the surface, element configurations, and existing states of elements in CDs-W and CDs-IL. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2b) of CDs-W and CDs-IL indicate various functionalities on their surfaces. Both exhibit characteristic absorption peaks at 3459–3129 (N–H, O–H), 1655 (–CONH–), 1607 (C]O), 1454, 1384 (C]C, C–H), 1312 (C–N), and 1040 cm−1 (C–O). The C–H bending vibration of the benzene ring is represented by the distinctive peaks at 829 and 792 cm−1. The stretching vibration of = CH was observed at 3064 cm−1 in the spectra of CDs, while the peaks at 2955, 2927, and 2850 cm−1 in spectra represent the C–H stretching [83–85]. The absorption peaks in CDs-IL FTIR spectra are much more complicated than CDs-W; for example, CDs-W has a sharp peak at 1384 cm−1 devoted to a dominated C–H bending of alkane. On the other side, CDs-IL has a sharp peak at 1516 cm−1, which is strongly devoted to N–O stretching, indicating that the CDs-IL prepared with EAN has a more complicated surface structure, as observed from CDs wavelength excitation dependency [86]. Due to the presence of polar functional groups on the surface has good solubility in water. Additionally, the two peaks in the Raman spectra at 1373 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 mimic disordered structures or defects (D band) and the graphitic domain (G band) corresponding to sp2 and sp3 carbon, respectively (Fig. 2c–d). The full Raman spectrum is shown in ESI (Fig. S2). The G band is connected to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice and corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite. The vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon are linked to the D band at about 1360 cm−1 [87]. CDs-W is more disorganized and amorphous than CDs-IL, as evidenced by the IG/ID indicating the degree in graphitization intensity ratios of 1.15 for CDs-W and 1.34 for CDs-IL, respectively [88]. The CDs-IL have the highest I G/I D value, which indicates a highly conjugated sp2 domain, possibly leading to the redshift of PL [33,89].

The fact is that the nitrogenated surface states in CDs-IL are greater than those in CDs-W; similarly, CDs-IL contains more oxygenated surface states than CDs-W, demonstrating the importance of the surface state configuration in the PL. The difference in solvent media would lead to a noticeable variation in the particle size and surface state of CDs. The possible PL mechanism prepared by IL and water can be proposed based on the results of various characterizations. We have studied the Zeta potential for doped CDs-IL to study the nature of their surfaces as well as their electron density. Zeta potential values for CDs-IL are found to be 14.6 mV indicating that positively charged functional groups are present at the surface of the CDs-IL. On the other hand, the Zeta potential of CDs-W is found to be -12.8 mV which indicates negatively charged surfaces.

The surface passivation/defect, surface state effect, heteroatom doping, edge state, and sp2 carbon-core state effect are the common elucidations for the long-wavelength and tunable PL of CDs [94,95]. The ‘molecular’ sp2 domains with a diameter of nearly 3 nm show red PL [96]. Here in CDs-IL, surface state effect, hetero atom doping, and Sp2 core state play a key role in the PL properties. Raman and XPS data are evidence for highly conjugated Sp2 core domain and surface functionality. CDs-IL with a smaller particle size distribution, as analyzed by TEM results, shows a longer wavelength PL, which is more extensive than CDs-W, which exhibits a shorter wavelength PL. In the undoped CDs systems, the energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) band gap is more elevated than heteroatom-doped CDs. Holak observed that nitrogen-doped CDs have a lower energy band gap, crucial to an apparent red shift in PL at the low energy in the visible light spectrum [97]. The PL of CDs is mainly due to conjugated atoms with electronegative atoms, which modulates the material HOMO and LUMO bandgap [98,99]. The electronegativity of oxygen and nitrogen is more robust, leading to a stronger trend of red-shifting in the PL of CDs. Therefore, a smaller particle size, graphite Ns, and increased oxygen and nitrogen content are all reasons for the redshift in the PL of CDs-IL. UV–vis, PL spectra with full width at half maximum, PLQYs, and other related data for both CDs have been analyzed in detail. The PLQYs for CDs-W and CDs-IL are 16.8 ± 2% to 68.6 ± 2%, respectively. In-situ surface modification using EAN generates white-light-emitting CDs showing a high PLQY compared to other white-light-emitting materials in ESI Table S1. Their excitation wavelengths were observed from 360 to 580 nm, PL wavelengths from 400 to 650 nm, and full width at half maximum at 116.5 and 103.0 nm (Fig. 4 b, e). UV–vis spectrums (Fig. 4a, d) of both CDs showed noticeable structural differences. CDs-IL shows longer wavelength UV–vis absorption and exhibited longer wavelengths PL. Here we clearly observe that IL plays a key role in the fabrication of CDs. The aggregation, dehydration, carbonization, core formation, and growth processes during the synthesis of CDs in IL are observed to change and the PL properties. ILs have features such as dehydration, high solubility, high boiling point, high polarity, and protic properties [100]. The polarity of IL renders a noticeable difference in the solvent and reactant interaction, enhancing the PL properties of CDs-IL.

The differences in the UV–vis spectrums of both CDs prove our inference (Fig. 4a, d). The peaks in the range 300–400 nm were usually caused by the n–π∗ transitions in the C–N or C–O bond, and absorption above 400 nm may be assigned for C]N absorption [101,102]. The nitrogen in 1,4-phenylenediamine possesses lone pair with a benzene ring showing absorption of the n–π∗ transitions [103]. In Uv–vis, as the polarity of the solvent increases, n-electrons of molecules form H-bonds interact via the n- π∗ transition, which causes the energy of the n track to decrease and the energy difference between the n and π∗ track to widen, causing the blue shifts in absorption. In EAN (the most robust polarity), its UV–vis spectra of CDs-IL show a blueshift in absorption [104]. The PL spectrums of both CDs were compared, showing excitation wavelength solid dependency (Fig. 4b, e). The CDs-IL with smaller particle sizes and more significant surface defects perform a leading role in PL by increasing the excitation wavelength. Both CDs contain an extensive range of particle sizes and surface defects, showing significant differences in PL. Quantum-confinement effect and surface state lead to the complexity of the excitation state of CDs-IL. CDs-IL shows dual emissive nature; the PL spectra show signatures in the blue and red emissive region of CDs-IL, further potential evidence for the presence of two PL centers. The blue emission vanished on excitation above 460 nm, and red PL is sustained at all excitations, which may be due to the carbon-core and molecular surface states [105–107]. As shown in the full PL spectra (ESI Figure S2), the mentioned wavelengths are used to excite these states. In addition, different core/surface states can be excited at different wavelengths and a notable difference in the white and red PL contributions. Carbon-core and molecular states are excited simultaneously at λex ≤ 420 nm favoring a stronger white PL. Conversely, longer excitation wavelengths (λex ≥ 440 nm) excited the lower HOMO–LUMO bandgap of the red PL component. Using the chromaticity function in the origin software, the excitation-dependent PL was confirmed by calculating the (Commission Internationale de I'Elcairage) CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates (x, y) from the PL data. However, the purity of the white-light emission (λex -360) changed dramatically at different excitation, as shown in Fig. 4c and f. It suggests that excitation wavelength plays a critical role in controlling the purity of white-light emission.

To understand the origin of the dynamic behavior of the PL in CDs-W and CDs-IL, time-resolved PL decay was recorded (shown in Fig. 5 ) and fitted using the exponential tail function I(t):

Where τ 1 and τ 2 indicate the shorter and longer lifetimes, respectively, and B 1 and B 2 are the amplitude of the relevant components at t = 0. The possible role of molecular state and second-order exponential components of an ensemble of emitting surface states in the PL decay are taken into consideration (n = 2). Average lifetimes for fit findings for CDs-W and CDs-IL were 4.91 ns and 8.32 ns, respectively, and other parameters were summarized in Table 2 . Roding et al. investigated the PL lifetimes of graphene QDs and divided them into two regions: the first homogeneous, like fluorescein dye system, and the second highly heterogeneous graphene QD system [108]. A variety of surface defects resulted in the excitation wavelength dependence PL. The PL decay of CDs with λ ex = 405 nm can be sub-characterized into two regions: the first is due to sp2 molecular states and the second is due to surface defects/core with much shorter τ values [109–111]. Molecular state exhibits the primary source of the absorption around 350 nm with a significant fraction of the total PL decay. Compared to the section of long-lived surface states in both CDs, the segment of molecular states with short PL decay periods is more substantial and is the cause of the enhanced PLQY. The long-lived surface defects are not as developed in CDs-W as they are in CDs-IL, which suggests that the environment and size of the π domains are more heterogeneous for the surface defects.

Our investigations showed that CDs-IL exhibits better PL properties than CDs-W and has more active surfaces/sites and functional groups. Therefore, we utilized CDs-IL as a sensor probe to detect water pollutants like aromatic nitro explosive compound 2,4,6 TNP and metal ions.

The aqueous-phase dispersion of CDs-IL reveals a strong fluorescence signature (Fig. 4e) that is due to conjugated, π-electron possessing π−π∗/n−π∗ transition and intra-functional groups charge transfer [112]. We pursued the PL behavior of CDs-IL as a probe toward harmful explosive TNP detection via the fluorometry method at room temperature. It is a surprise that CDs-IL showed 96.17 ± 2% quenching on the addition of 500 μM TNP solution (Fig. 6 a). In addition to titration tests, a decrease in PL intensity was seen in a UV chamber (365 nm), where the whitish PL of CDs-IL in the cuvette immediately faded upon adding the TNP stock solution. Stern-Volmer (S–V) equation, (F0/F = A.Ek[m] + B, where [m] is the molar concentration of the analyte (quencher), F 0 and F are the PL intensities of the pure CDs-IL dispersion prior to and following the addition of analyte, and K SV (quenching constant) is the product of constants A and k used in the non-linear quenching was used to calculate the amount of PL quenching of CDs-IL by TNP. The concurrent occurrence of static and dynamic quenching is supported by the non-linear S–V curve at higher TNP concentrations (Fig. 6c). K SV was determined to be 6.78103 M−1 from the S–V plot by exponential fitting. We then investigated the sensing capability of CDs-IL toward dangerous nitroaromatic explosives such as 2,4,6-TNP after being encouraged by such ultrasensitive detection. Further, the limit of detection (LOD) [113] was calculated from equation 3σ/s using a 100 μM stock solution of TNP, where σ is the standard deviation of five consecutive blank measurements of the PL intensity of CDs-IL at regular time intervals (ESI Fig. S4), and s is the slope of the curve of the decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs-IL vs. volume of added analyte (Fig. 6a). The LOD is obtained with the linear fitting of calibration for analytes (ESI-Fig. S5). Gratifyingly, the LOD value of TNP is found to be 0.049 ± 0.005 μM. In view of the nanomolar level of detection, such LOD for TNP is excellent among all CD sensors reported so far, as shown in ESI Table S2.

The use of CDs in sensing hazardous metals in an aqueous medium due to their potential in ultrasensitive and quick-responding PL sensing of diverse metal ions contaminations. Consequently, we chose different metals salts like Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Ca2+ (concentration: 2 mM). PL titrations of CDs-IL with every metal ion were performed with the addition of 500 μl (shown in ESI Figure S3). Amazingly, adding 1000 μM of Fe3+ solution decreases the PL intensity of CDs-IL above 85 ± 2% (Fig. 6b), coupled with a noticeable colorimetric shift of the solution under UV illumination. The remaining metal ions, however, experience barely any alteration. The PL intensity of CDs-IL decreases gradually with the increasing concentration of Fe3+ revealing its quenching effect. Quantifying quenching for CDs-IL revealed that the K SV value of 1.08 × 103 M−1 exceeds most of the well-reported Fe3+ sensory CDs-IL in H2O (ESI Table S3) and indicates a strong interaction of Fe3+ with CDs-IL. The LOD is obtained with the linear fitting of calibration for analytes (ESI-Fig. S6). The LOD was calculated to be a remarkable 0.117 ± 0.01 μM and truly corroborates the ultra-sensitivity of CDs-IL toward Fe3+ detection.

In the case of TNP, according to ESI Figure S7, there is little to no overlap between the PL of CDs-IL and UV spectrums of TNP, ruling out the potential of fluorescence resonance energy transfer [114–116]. With a potent nitro group strongly drawing electrons, TNP is the typical electron-deficient nitroaromatic, which engages electron-rich amino groups and fluorescence quenching occurs via a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, forming a Meisenheimer complex with CDs-IL [117,118]. This is supported by Zeta potential analysis, the zeta potential of CDs-IL is 14.6 mV, but after the addition of TNP, it decreases significantly to 6.2 mV. TNP is electron-deficient nitro compound, it is quickly to be attached to the CDs-IL resulting in such strong PL quenching via PET mechanism. As a result of CDs-IL, some other levels will be formed near the conduction band among which the transition has taken place. During the PET process, when TNP is gradually added to this CDs-IL solution, PL spectra are quenched. We also studied the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of CDs-IL in the absence and presence of TNP, which confirms the static quenching mechanism between CDs-IL and TNP (ESI Figure S8). Average lives of 8.32 ns and 7.98 ns, respectively, the fluorescence lifetime curves of CDs-IL and CDs-IL/TNP fit a second-order exponential function. According to the findings, there is no significant change in lifetime decay that confirms the static quenching process may be responsible for the PL quenching.

CDs-ILs established coordination bonds between Fe3+ via amino, hydroxyl, or amide groups and the quenching process occurred in this situation. Due to the disruption of the radiative transition, PL quenching occurs, which has response characteristics comparable to those of graphitic CDs. The PL quenching process between Fe3+ and the CDs is further demonstrated by evaluating the kinetics of the process. The zeta potential of CDs-IL decreased to 1.01 mV after the addition of Fe3+. According to ESI Fig S7, the average lives of CDs-IL and CDs-IL/Fe3+ PL lifetime curves, which are revealed to be 8.32 ns and 5.73 ns, respectively, fit a second-order exponential function. While their lifetimes have barely altered, PL spectrums have (Fig. 6) changed significantly, suggesting that the quenching mechanism of CDs-IL in the presence of TNP and Fe3+ is static in nature [76].

ITC of CDs-IL with TNP and Fe3+ in aqueous media were performed, where changes in heat peak after each injection of analytes imply the exothermic for TNP and endothermic binding for Fe3+ to CDs-IL (Fig. 8). At thermodynamic equilibrium, the heat change amount in the molar ratio of analytes to CDs-IL is almost zero [119]. The ITC data fit the one-site model well to calculate the enthalpy changes that analytes coordinate with CDs-IL molecules in the presence of water. TNP binds with CDs-IL during titrations, and complexation is exothermic (ΔH = −97.3 kJ mol−1) with a calculated ΔG of -22.5 kJ mol−1 at 25 °C. In the case of Fe3+ titrations, Fe3+ ions are located in distorted octahedral complexes, some with oxygenated groups and some with nitrogenated groups of the CDs-IL. The complexation of Fe3+ with CDs-IL is strongly endothermic (ΔH = 1.26 × 105 kJ mol−1) with a calculated ΔG of -9.48 × 102 kJ mol−1 at 25 °C. In both cases, the negative ΔG indicates that TNP and Fe3+ binding with CDs-IL is a spontaneous process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory activity of CDs on bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: The bacterial inhibition activity of CDs was examined using 100 ppm stock dispersion. We observed no inhibition growth on all three bacterial cells of CDs-IL and CDs-W (ESI Figure S9). This result clearly shows CDs are not toxic to bacterial cells, which can act as sustainable utilization of CDs in many sensing applications by replacing toxic dyes like Ethidium bromide (EtBr), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), and green fluorescent protein (GFP). Similar task-specific ILs show minimum inhibitory activity on bacteria cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging studies of CDs-treated bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high PL performance of CDs, we investigated imaging applications on bacterial cells. The epifluorescence photomicrography of bacterial cells was examined for both CDs on stained bacterial cells under bright and PL light. The bioimages of both bacterial cells (Vibrio sp. and Pseudomonas sp.) showed a good PL activity at the Rhodamine filter with an excitation of 633 nm (Fig. 9 b, 9b∗). Here, we observe that bacterial cells treated with CD-IL showed high intense emission (Fig. 9b, b∗) than those treated with CD-W (Fig. 9c, c∗). This PL activity of CDs on bacterial cells is due to the electrostatic interaction because of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation in bacterial cells. These electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions and hydrogen bonding with the negatively charged bacterial cell wall help to penetrate CDs successfully inside the bacterial cells and are easily detectable in fluorescent microscopy [120,121], even after both CDs treatments have not shown any such inhibition on ROS of bacterial cells (ESI Figure S6). The result also confirmed that both CDs were easily internalized in bacterial cells and did not inhibit ROS due to high fluorescence activity in images. We stained both bacterial cultures with CDs only for 15 min before bioimaging in epifluorescence microscopy. Many studies have shown fluorescence activity after overnight-grown bacterial culture in various CDs concentration [77].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acute toxicity analysis of CDs on the Artemia salina
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity analysis of both CDs was performed on the animal model (Artemia salina), which is widely used and is an acceptable alternate model for mammalian cells [122]. The mortality of A. salina was determined based on different concentrations of CDs over time (Fig. 10 ).

We have not observed any mortality of A. salina at a 500 ppm concentration of CDs-IL till 24 h of incubation. In contrast, CDs-W has shown similar results at 500 ppm concentration with a meagre mortality rate of 8.33%. More than 60% mortality of A. salina was observed at further higher concentrations (1000 ppm) of both CDs after 24 h. Compared to the control, the difference in mortality of CDs-IL was slightly higher, which indicates, to some extent, toxic in nature with a higher concentration of CDs-IL on Artemia salina. The mechanism for the toxicity of CDs on Artemia salina is mainly a combined effect that is mostly by oxidative stress, agglomeration, and physical interactions, including oxidative stress by producing ROS, which alters the antioxidant defense system, such as superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase (GPx), and catalase. Due to CDs chemical properties, ROS are reactive with different biological molecules, causing oxidative damage to proteins, lipids, and DNA. The inhibition of swimming may be caused by the malformation of gills by the attachment of nanomaterials to the gills [123]. CDs were able to penetrate into the cell membrane; Artemia also has an affinity for bio-accumulation of this type of different heavy metal ions, mainly in the gastrointestinal tract. The uptake of CDs was studied in relation to food supplements. CDs accumulation in the digestive tract will lower the presence of food [124].

In summary, CDs-IL with white PL properties was synthesized by a solvothermal method by regulating N-doping content using an IL EAN. CDs-IL, with a high PLQY of 68.6 ± 2%, has shown broad-spectrum absorption and wide emission response (FWHM = 103 nm). This unique PL characteristic of CDs-IL originates from the synergistic effect of surface defects and high N-doping. The PL characteristics of CDs-IL have been shown to have significant potential in bioimaging, white-light-emitting display systems, and advanced information encryption. In one of these uses, the CDs-IL serves as a luminous probe at a long excitation wavelength (633 nm) for imaging of bacterial cells. CDs-IL also shows excellent sensing capability for TNP and Fe3+ with LOD 0.049 ± 0.005 μM, 0.117 ± 0.01 μM, respectively. This research focuses on white luminescent materials based on CDs that can be regulated by solvent choice, offering a practical method for designing and fabricating new classes of white luminescent materials that could broaden their applications in photocatalysts, bioimaging, and solar cells.

Sanjay Mehra: Synthesis and characterization of the materials, investigation in sensing, writing the original draft. Shrikant D Khandare: bio-imaging, data visualization, reviewing, editing. Kuldeep Singh: Data correction, Reviewing, Editing. Doongar R Chaudhary: bioimaging supervision, reviewing, editing. Arvind Kumar: Conceptualization, methodology, writing the original draft, supervision reviewing, editing.
